name: Atari 520ST
description:
Step into the vibrant world of the 1980s with the Atari 520ST, a groundbreaking home computer that defied expectations. Unveiled in 1985, the 520ST wasn't just powerful for its time, it was revolutionary. Packing aMotorola 68000 processor, it boasted dazzling graphics and a smooth-operating Graphical User Interface (GUI) – a rarity back then. Imagine navigating menus and clicking on icons instead of typing cryptic commands! This user-friendly design made computing accessible to a wider audience, from hobbyists to budding artists and musicians.

But the 520ST wasn't just about looks. Its powerful sound chip and high-resolution display breathed life into games like "Falcon Patrol" and "Dungeon Master," immersing players in fantastical worlds. The machine also excelled in creative pursuits. With MIDI ports and music composition software, the 520ST became a haven for aspiring musicians, birthing legendary soundtracks like the one for "The Secret of Monkey Island." Its capabilities even extended to desktop publishing, allowing users to design newsletters and flyers with professional flair.

Despite its innovations, the 520ST's reign wasn't eternal. Competition from Commodore and Amiga, coupled with Atari's own internal struggles, eventually dimmed its star. Yet, the legacy of this pioneering machine lives on. For many, it was a gateway to the digital world, sparking a lifelong passion for technology and creativity. So, take a moment to appreciate the Atari 520ST, a true star in the home computing galaxy that dared to be different, and in doing so, opened new doors for a generation.

name: Coomber 6000 Series
description:
The Coomber 6000 series is a portable audio system from a bygone era, likely produced in the late 1980s or early 1990s. It's a two-in-one device, combining a CD player and a cassette tape player to cater to the diverse music tastes of the time. Think of it as a precursor to the MP3 players and smartphones that rule today's audio landscape.

Imagine a small, rectangular box with built-in speakers, roughly the size of a large hardback book. The CD player would likely have a tray on top where you'd slot your discs, while the cassette deck would be tucked away behind a door or flap. Buttons and dials would control playback, volume, and other functions. Think of it as a simpler version of a modern boombox, without the fancy features or booming bass.

While not exactly cutting-edge technology by today's standards, the Coomber 6000 series served as a reliable source of entertainment for its time. It could be plugged into the mains for home use or powered by batteries for portable tunes on picnics or camping trips. For those who grew up with the hiss of cassette tapes and the satisfying click of a CD changing tracks, the Coomber 6000 series represents a nostalgic piece of audio history, a reminder of simpler times when music choices were more tangible and portable entertainment wasn't confined to our pockets.

name: Hamburger Phone
description:
Ah, the vintage hamburger phone, a quirky icon of the 90s that brings back a wave of nostalgia! Imagine a bright red patty for the base, topped with a glossy green lettuce dial pad, a sesame seed ring button, and a melty yellow cheese receiver cradled on top. These playful phones weren't just conversation starters, they were functional landline telephones, complete with a curly cord and satisfyingly chunky buttons.

While the exact origins remain debatable, hamburger phones exploded in popularity during the 1990s, fueled by the rise of pop culture and a general embrace of kitschy aesthetics. They adorned bedrooms, kitchens, and even office desks, adding a touch of whimsy to everyday life. Kids loved the playful design, while adults couldn't resist a device that was equal parts useful and conversation starter. Remember the first time you picked up that cheesy receiver and heard the muffled "hello" from your best friend? The anticipation, the excitement, it was all part of the hamburger phone experience.

Though their reign as the must-have gadget faded with the 2000s, hamburger phones remain a beloved symbol of a simpler time. They're a reminder that technology can be fun, that design can be playful, and that a good phone call can bring a smile to your face. So next time you stumble upon one at a vintage store or flea market, take a moment to appreciate its chunky charm. Who knows, it might just inspire you to pick up the receiver and dial up a friend for a good old-fashioned chat.

name: Apple iBook G3
description: Step into the time machine and travel back to 1999 when Apple unveiled its iconic iBook G3, a laptop that combined cutting-edge technology with a dash of colorful whimsy. The iBook G3, affectionately known as the "Clamshell" due to its distinctive design resembling a colorful clamshell, was a breath of fresh air in the tech world. Boasting a 12.1-inch display, a speedy PowerPC G3 processor, and a curvaceous, translucent plastic exterior, it was a fashion statement as much as a computing powerhouse. Apple gave users the freedom to express their individuality by offering the iBook G3 in an array of vibrant colors, from Tangerine to Blueberry, turning heads in coffee shops and classrooms alike. Equipped with a handle for easy portability and a robust design that could withstand the bumps and bruises of everyday life, the iBook G3 wasn't just a laptop; it was a stylish companion for the tech-savvy adventurer. This iconic piece of tech history not only marked a turning point in laptop design but also became a beloved relic for those who fondly remember the days when their computing world was a little more colorful. Apple's iBook G3 Introduction Video - Launched 1999

name: Apple iMac G3
description:
A revolutionary computer that burst onto the scene in 1998, bringing with it a wave of nostalgia that persists even today. Imagine a world dominated by beige, boxy PCs, then picture this: a translucent Bondi blue bubble housing a monitor, CPU, and all the essential tech, perched atop a sleek, curved stand. That was the iMac G3, a bold departure from the norm that redefined what a personal computer could look like.

But the G3 wasn't just a pretty face. It was packed with innovative features for its time. It booted up the colorful world of Mac OS, boasted a speedy PowerPC G3 processor, and even ditched the floppy disk drive for the then-futuristic USB ports. No more wrestling with finicky disks – just plug and play! And let's not forget the iconic translucent keyboard and mouse, adding to the iMac's playful, all-in-one charm.

The iMac G3 wasn't just a computer; it was a statement. It dared to be different, to be fun, and to be accessible. It ushered in a new era of Apple design, one that prioritized user experience and aesthetics alongside technical prowess. And while its specs might seem quaint by today's standards, the G3's impact on the tech world, and our collective memory, is undeniable. So, next time you see that cheerful blue bubble, remember the iMac G3 – the computer that dared to be different, and in doing so, changed everything.

name: Apple iMac G4
description:
Remember the days of dial-up screeching and bulky beige boxes? Enter the iMac G4, a 2002 revolution that wasn't just a computer. This all-in-one beauty ditched the CRT clunk for a vibrant 15-inch LCD display, housed in a translucent, head-turning shell. Forget hiding it under the desk, the G4 demanded center stage, declaring: "I'm different, and I'm here to stay!"

But the G4 wasn't just a looker. Its PowerPC G4 processor hummed with power, leaving sluggish Pentiums in the dust. It became the creative's best friend, editing photos, composing music, and even birthing those iconic bouncing DVD logos. Packed with ports for everything from printers to scanners to that fancy new iPod, it was a connectivity hub for the digital age. And let's not forget the software symphony - the G4 ushered in the user-friendly Mac OS X, making computing accessible for all.

The G4 wasn't just a machine; it was a cultural phenomenon. It graced the desks of artists, celebrities, and everyday people alike, even starring in movies like "Finding Nemo." It embodied the cool, innovative spirit of Apple, proving that tech could be beautiful, user-friendly, and yes, even desirable. So, the next time you see a sleek iMac, remember its bulbous predecessor, the Bondi Blue Colossus, a machine that dared to be different and changed the world, one pixel at a time.

name: Apple MacBook (mid-2007)
description:
Released in May 2007, the MacBook (mid-2007) wasn't a major revamp, but rather a refresh of the late 2006 model. Think of it as the iPhone 13 to the 12: mostly the same, but with a few key improvements.

The biggest change was the processor. Apple swapped the Core Duo in the earlier model for a zippier Intel Core 2 Duo. This meant faster performance for everyday tasks like browsing the web, writing documents, and even editing photos. Imagine going from a car with a four-cylinder engine to one with a six-cylinder –– things just felt smoother and quicker.

Other upgrades included a slightly larger hard drive (up to 160GB) and improved Wi-Fi support with the new 802.11n standard. This meant faster internet speeds, especially if you had a compatible router at home. Plus, the keyboard received a tweak, gaining multimedia control keys like play, pause, and volume. Overall, the MacBook (mid-2007) was a solid update, offering better performance and usability without reinventing the wheel.

However, it's important to remember that this is a 17-year-old laptop by now. While it can still handle basic tasks, it won't be able to run the latest software or perform resource-intensive activities like video editing.

name: Apple Powerbook 520
description:
Step into the technological time capsule and marvel at the Apple PowerBook 520, a true gem from the early '90s that redefined the world of portable computing. With its sleek design and cutting-edge features, the PowerBook 520 stood as a symbol of innovation and sophistication. Encased in a compact and stylish chassis, it boasted a vivid 9.8-inch grayscale display that brought clarity to the fingertips of users. The keyboard, a tactile masterpiece, provided a responsive typing experience, making every keystroke a pleasure.

Underneath its sleek exterior, the PowerBook 520 was a powerhouse of its era, equipped with a 25 MHz Motorola 68LC040 processor that delivered snappy performance for its time. The technological marvel didn't stop there – it featured a remarkable 4 MB of RAM, providing users with the multitasking capabilities that were revolutionary in the early '90s. The PowerBook 520 allowed users to seamlessly navigate the digital landscape, making it a coveted companion for professionals and enthusiasts alike.

As a museum piece, the Apple PowerBook 520 stands as a testament to the evolution of portable computing. Its compact form factor, innovative design, and groundbreaking features showcase an era when the boundaries of technology were being pushed. Visitors to the exhibit can't help but be captivated by the nostalgia and the pioneering spirit embodied in this iconic piece of computing history.

name: Apple Powerbook G3
description:
Step into the power-packed era of the late '90s, where the Apple PowerBook G3 takes center stage as a technological luminary that pushed the boundaries of portable computing. Picture this: a sleek and curvaceous design that exudes sophistication, housing a powerhouse of innovation beneath its stylish exterior. The PowerBook G3 was a symbol of Apple's commitment to blending form and function seamlessly, captivating tech enthusiasts with its celestial allure.

At the heart of this captivating piece of tech wizardry was the PowerPC G3 processor, a lightning-fast engine that propelled the PowerBook G3 into the realms of computing supremacy. It was the era when having a laptop wasn't just about utility; it was about making a statement. The PowerBook G3 was a statement in itself, boasting a 14.1-inch display that rendered colors with cinematic vibrancy. Imagine the awe-inspiring moment when users lifted the "lid" to reveal not just a computer but a work of art.

But what truly set the PowerBook G3 apart was its groundbreaking "Wall Street" design, featuring a lustrous bronze keyboard and a sleek metallic finish that screamed sophistication. This technological marvel wasn't just a laptop; it was a status symbol, an embodiment of the late '90s tech zeitgeist. As you encounter the PowerBook G3 in the museum, let your imagination soar back to an era when computing met glamour, and the PowerBook G3 stood as the vanguard of a digital revolution that would shape the future of portable technology.

name: Kodak DC3200
description:
Transport yourself back to the turn of the millennium, where the Kodak DC3200 was the snapshot sensation of 1999. This digital gem was more than just a camera; it was a gateway to the future of photography. With a 1.2-megapixel sensor, the DC3200, armed with its cutting-edge 2 MB of RAM (today, that is tiny), was a pocket-sized powerhouse that brought the joys of digital imaging to amateur photographers.

Its compact design and user-friendly interface made it a hit for capturing life's candid moments without the hassle of film rolls and darkrooms. The 1.8-inch LCD display was a technological marvel at the time, allowing users to review their shots on the spot. The DC3200 may seem quaint compared to today's camera behemoths, but in its heyday, it was a revolutionary step toward the digital photography era. So, here's to the Kodak DC3200, a pixelated pioneer that made memories tangible with just a click.

name: Nokia 3310
description:
The Nokia 3310, released in 2000, wasn't just a phone, it was a legend. Back in the flip-phone era, it stood out for its simplicity and, most notably, its legendary durability. Remember those brick-sized Nokias? The 3310 was one of them, built with a tough plastic body that seemed impervious to drops, spills, and even the occasional accidental fling across the room. Stories abound of these phones surviving being run over by cars, used as hammers, and even taking a dip in the washing machine, all emerging unscathed. This near-indestructibility earned the 3310 a cult following and a reputation as the phone that could literally take a beating.

But the 3310 wasn't just tough, it was practical. Its battery life was epic, lasting for days on a single charge. The interface was straightforward, with easy-to-navigate menus and a classic Snake game that kept millions entertained for hours. It did calls and texts flawlessly, without the distractions or complexities of smartphones. In a world of ever-changing technology, the 3310 offered a reassuring sense of permanence, a reliable connection to the outside world without the bells and whistles that could drain your battery and your attention.

Though the original 3310 is no longer in production, its legacy lives on. Newer versions capture the spirit of the original, offering the same no-nonsense functionality with a few modern upgrades. But the spirit of the original 3310, the phone that could survive anything and keep you connected to what mattered most, remains a testament to the power of simplicity and durability in a world often obsessed with the latest tech.

name: Nokia 2610
description:
The Nokia 2610, released in 2006, wasn't your average cell phone. Forget sleek smartphones and touchscreens; this bad boy was all about basic communication, built to last. Imagine a chunky candy bar with a small, color screen and a satisfyingly clicky button pad. It could make calls, send texts, play Snake (the OG mobile game!), and even record voice memos. No fancy apps, no social media, just pure fun!

But the 2610 wasn't just about features. It was about legendary durability. Remember those "drop test" videos where phones shattered like glass? Not the 2610. This phone could tumble down stairs, survive accidental dunkings in the sink, and even bounce off concrete without a scratch. It felt like it was made of something stronger than plastic, maybe a mythical Nokia-ium alloy. People joked that you could use it as a hammer in a pinch, and honestly, it probably wouldn't break. The 2610 was the ultimate companion for the clumsy or adventurous, a phone that could handle whatever life threw at it (or you accidentally dropped it on).

So, while the 2610 might seem outdated by today's standards, it holds a special place in tech history. It was a reminder that phones didn't need to be complicated to be reliable and fun. And, of course, it was basically indestructible, a testament to the "built to last" mentality that made Nokia a household name. In a world of fragile smartphones, the 2610 stands as a rugged icon, a reminder of simpler times and phones that could truly take a beating.

name: PlayStation 1
description:
Ah, the PlayStation 1! A true gaming legend that brought 3D worlds and epic adventures into living rooms around the globe. Released in 1994, it wasn't just a console; it was a revolution.

Imagine ditching clunky cartridges for sleek, shiny CDs. These discs held massive games with mind-blowing 3D graphics that made you feel like you were stepping right into the action. Soar through the skies as Crash Bandicoot, race futuristic hovercrafts in Wipeout, or solve puzzles in the haunting world of Tomb Raider. Each game was a portal to a new and exciting reality.

But the PlayStation 1 wasn't just about flashy graphics. It was about the iconic controller, the DualShock. Gone were the stiff buttons of the past. This baby vibrated in your hands, rumbled with explosions, and even had two analog sticks for ultimate control. It felt like you were really part of the game, not just watching it unfold on the screen.

So, next time you see that classic gray console, remember the impact it had. The PlayStation 1 wasn't just a toy; it was a cultural phenomenon that changed the way we play games forever. And who knows, maybe you'll even be inspired to dust off your old copy of Spyro the Dragon and relive the magic for yourself!

name: PlayStation 2
description:
This device can be seen with various other game boxes for the PS2 in the tech museum.

It was developed by Sony Interactive Entertainment. The first release of the product was based in Japan, on the 4th March 2000, and in North America on the 26th October 2000, amongst many other places.

Obviously the PlayStation 2 is the successor to the original PlayStation, and was the second installment in the PlayStation brand of game consoles. This product faced many challenges, competitive wise.. due to it being in a sixth-generation console, it had so many competitors such as Nintendo's GameCube, Sega's Dreamcast, and Microsoft's Xbox lineup.

Being the best-selling video game console of all time, in total it had sold over 155 million units worldwide, completely thrashing the numbers of the Dreamcast, Xbox and Gamecube all in one go.

Broadcasted by Sony worldwide in 1999, they announced they would be continuing the PlayStation series lineup, and creating the second product in the series.. the PlayStation 2. Because of all the excitement, over 4,000 games were developed for the device.

Sony decided they needed a more slim, lighter PlayStation so, based of the PS2 design, they created the PS2 Slim. Because everybody liked the PS2 Slim so much, it carried it's popularity pretty far into the seventh generation, even after the PlayStation 3 was released.

After over twelve whole years, production stopped for the PlayStation 2 in 2013. Remember, this was one of the longest ever lifespans for any video game console. Games continued to be developed and released way after the product's production and support had been discontinued.

This just goes to show how much the gaming community appreciated this phenomenal device.

name: Sony Handycam
description:
Step into the annals of video recording history and witness the marvel that is the Sony Handycam, an iconic relic from the era when home video capture first became accessible to the masses. The Sony Handycam, with its compact and ergonomic design, brought the power of video recording directly into the hands of enthusiasts and families alike. Its diminutive size belied its technological prowess, making it a pioneer in the realm of consumer camcorders.

Equipped with a cutting-edge CCD sensor, the Sony Handycam captured moments with vivid clarity, preserving memories in a format that was revolutionary for its time. The integrated 8x optical zoom lens allowed users to zoom in on the details of life's precious moments, whether it was a child's first steps or a family gathering. The ease of use was further enhanced by the flip-out LCD screen, providing a live view of the recording and playback functionality – a feature that was groundbreaking in the realm of consumer electronics.

A testament to its innovation, the Sony Handycam symbolizes an era when video recording transitioned from a niche endeavor to an everyday practice. With the ability to record directly onto convenient and compact tapes, this handheld wonder transformed the way we documented our lives. As a cherished exhibit in the museum, the Sony Handycam evokes a sense of nostalgia, reminding visitors of the bygone era when capturing life's moments on video became a tangible reality for the masses.

name: Xbox 360
description: The Xbox 360 is a video game console part of the Xbox series brand developed by Microsoft. This was a successor to the original Xbox, which is also in our tech museum! If you haven’t seen it on our page, check it out here. The PlayStation 3 developed by Sony, and Nintendo’s Wii were the obvious competitors with the Xbox 360. This second console in the Xbox series featured an online service, Xbox Live, which was expanded from its previous version on the original Xbox. Xbox live received regular updates like any service during the console’s lifetime. This service allowed players to play online games, download games through the Xbox Live Arcade, and download game demos to try out games that they may like to purchase at some time. It also enabled them to easily purchase and stream music directly from their Xbox console, play television programs and much more. Numerous peripheral devices have been released to be compatible with the Xbox 360, including wireless controllers, expanded hard drive storage to store more games, and the different types of files for the services Xbox Live can provide as explained above, and the Kinect motion sensing camera. The Xbox 360 was released around 2005 and its lifespan went to about 2016, which is a pretty good amount of time for a console to last for! Of course, it’s operating system was a custom one, the Xbox 360 System Software OS, and the machine using a 3.2 GHz PowerPC Tri-Core Xenon CPU and using 512 MB of unified GDDR3 RAM clocked at 700 MHz. Note that there was also 10MB of eDRAM (embedded DRAM, integrated on the same multi-chip module (MCM), which allows for much higher operation speeds) cache on the Xenos GPU.

name: Xbox Original
description: The Crystal Edition Xbox was first released in North America in 2001, the aim was to celebrate the console's second anniversary in the region. The box bundle contained the console and two matching crystal controllers. However, a few weeks later, the bundle was made available in many Canadian stores. Variants of the Crystal Edition were sold in stores bundles with different games at later dates. This Xbox you're viewing only had a lifespan of 5 years! There were 24 million units sold in total (as of May 10 2006). This model only had about 64 MB of DDR SDRAM @ 200 MHz and an 8 or 10 GB internal hard drive along with an 8MB memory card. The best selling game that was used here was Halo 2, which has a record of 8.46 million users (as of November 2008). The successor to the first generation of the Xbox was the Xbox 360, which you may have seen already. If not, click here.

name: ZX Spectrum
description:
The ZX Spectrum is an 8-bit home computer. It's main design was focused around being small, portable, simple and have a low overall cost. It was conceived and designed by English entrepreneur and inventor Clive Sinclair, who was well known for his eccentricity and pioneering.

The design came from the name "Spectrum", as the colour spectrum, as you can see the rainbow on the right side of the device.

During the release, it was first shipped out via mail, but then after the issue of severe backlogs, the machine was sold through High Street chains in the United Kingdom.

Sadly, this device was discontinued in 1992, but still sold a whopping 5 million models!
